runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity for prime-age U.S. workers already employed, holding marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding extensive-margin (participation) responses, used in lifecycle or macro calibration.
citations: MaCurdy (1981) – early lifecycle estimate placing intensive-margin Frisch elasticity around 0.1–0.45 for prime-age men
Chetty (2012, AER) – reconciliation paper arguing micro Frisch elasticities of ~0.5 are consistent with macro moments once optimization frictions are accounted for
Keane & Rogerson (2012, JEL) – survey noting wide range of estimates but a central tendency near 0.3–0.5 for intensive margin
runs.reasoningSummary: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity for employed prime-age U.S. workers is one of the most studied parameters in labor economics. Micro studies (e.g., MaCurdy 1981, Altonji 1986) typically find estimates in the 0.1–0.5 range for men. Macro-calibration papers (e.g., Chetty 2012) suggest values closer to 0.5 remain defensible once frictions are incorporated. Surveys (Keane & Rogerson 2012) document a central tendency around 0.3–0.5. My median is 0.4, reflecting the bulk of credible micro evidence. I assign a fairly wide spread (p05=0.1, p95=0.9) because estimates vary considerably across identification strategies, samples, and time periods, and debate about the "true" structural parameter is ongoing.

Micro studies (e.g., MaCurdy 1981, Altonji 1986) typically find estimates in the 0.1\u20130.5 range for men. Macro-calibration papers (e.g., Chetty 2012) suggest values closer to 0.5 remain defensible once frictions are incorporated. Surveys (Keane & Rogerson 2012) document a central tendency around 0.3\u20130.5. My median is 0.4, reflecting the bulk of credible micro evidence. I assign a fairly wide spread (p05=0.1, p95=0.9) because estimates vary considerably across identification strategies, samples, and time periods, and debate about the \"true\" structural parameter is ongoing."}

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin Frisch elasticity of labor supply: percent change in annual hours worked for prime-age employed U.S. workers from a 1% change in the net-of-tax wage, holding the marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding extensive-margin (participation) responses, as used in lifecycle or macro calibration.
citations: MaCurdy (1981, JPE) — structural lifecycle estimate of intensive-margin Frisch elasticity around 0.1–0.45 for prime-age men
Chetty (2012, AEJ:Macro) — meta-analysis reconciling micro and macro estimates; preferred intensive-margin Frisch around 0.5 after adjustment
Keane & Rogerson (2012, JEL) — review arguing macro-consistent Frisch elasticity is higher (~0.5–1.0) once human capital and extensive margins are accounted for
runs.reasoningSummary: Micro-econometric studies of prime-age men's hours (e.g., MaCurdy 1981) typically yield intensive-margin Frisch elasticities in the 0.1–0.45 range. Chetty's (2012) reconciliation, correcting for optimization frictions and measurement error, pushes preferred estimates toward 0.5. Macro-calibration literature (e.g., Keane & Rogerson) often uses values of 0.5–1.0, but much of that reflects extensive-margin or human-capital channels excluded here. Restricting to intensive-margin hours for already-employed prime-age workers, I center at 0.4, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.1 to 0.9 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

citations: MaCurdy (1981) – seminal lifecycle labor supply paper estimating intensive-margin Frisch elasticities around 0.1–0.45 for men using PSID data.
Domeij & Flodén (2006) – correcting for borrowing constraints raises male Frisch elasticity estimates toward 0.5–0.7.

Chetty (2012, JPE) – meta-analysis reconciling micro and macro estimates, argues a Frisch elasticity of ~0.5 on the intensive margin is consistent with micro evidence after accounting for optimization frictions.
runs.reasoningSummary: The micro labor-supply literature (MaCurdy 1981 and successors) typically finds intensive-margin Frisch elasticities for prime-age men in the range 0.1–0.5, with some estimates for women higher. Corrections for measurement error and borrowing constraints (Domeij & Flodén 2006) push estimates upward. Chetty (2012) argues the micro-consistent intensive-margin value is around 0.5. Macro calibrations often use 0.5–1.0. Given the definition excludes participation and focuses on prime-age workers, I center my belief around 0.5, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.1–1.1 across the 5th–95th percentile range.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity for prime-age U.S. workers already employed, holding marginal utility of wealth constant, excluding extensive-margin (participation) responses, as used in lifecycle or macro calibration.
citations: MaCurdy (1981, JPE) — seminal lifecycle estimate of Frisch elasticity around 0.1–0.45 for males
Chetty (2012, AEJ: Macro) — meta-analysis reconciling micro and macro estimates, preferred intensive-margin value ~0.5
Keane & Rogerson (2012, JEL) — survey arguing macro-consistent Frisch elasticity on intensive margin around 0.5–1.0 when aggregation issues addressed
runs.reasoningSummary: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity for prime-age U.S. workers is a well-studied but contested parameter. Micro-econometric studies (e.g., MaCurdy 1981) tend to yield estimates in the 0.1–0.45 range for men. Chetty's (2012) meta-analysis places a preferred intensive-margin value near 0.5 after correcting for optimization frictions. Keane and Rogerson (2012) argue that properly accounting for aggregation and heterogeneity pushes estimates somewhat higher. The consensus for macro calibration of the intensive margin (excluding participation) typically lands in the 0.3–0.6 range, with significant uncertainty on both sides. I set p50 = 0.4, reflecting the central tendency of micro-to-medium estimates, with a right-skewed distribution since macro modelers sometimes use values up to ~1.0.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity: percent change in annual hours worked by already-employed prime-age U.S. workers from a 1% change in the net-of-tax wage, holding the marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation (extensive-margin) responses; used in lifecycle or macro calibration.
citations: MaCurdy (1981) – structural lifecycle estimate of the Frisch elasticity for male workers, often cited around 0.1–0.45
Chetty (2012, AER) – meta-analysis reconciling micro and macro estimates; argues intensive-margin Frisch elasticity around 0.5 with optimization frictions
Keane (2011, JEL) – survey arguing true Frisch elasticity is higher (~0.5–1.0) once human capital and other margins are accounted for
runs.reasoningSummary: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity for prime-age employed U.S. workers is the subject of longstanding debate. Structural micro estimates (MaCurdy-style lifecycle models) typically yield values of 0.1–0.45 for men, with some studies near zero. Chetty (2012) argues that adjustment costs in micro data compress observed elasticities and that the corrected intensive-margin value is around 0.5. Macro/calibration practice often uses values in the 0.25–0.5 range. Excluding participation and focusing strictly on hours conditional on employment pushes estimates toward the lower end relative to broader measures. I center my belief near 0.3, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.75 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

runs.reasoningSummary: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity for prime-age employed U.S. workers is a long-debated parameter. Pure microeconometric studies (e.g., MaCurdy 1981) tend to yield estimates in the 0.1–0.45 range for men. Chetty's (2012) reconciliation of micro and macro evidence places a corrected intensive-margin estimate around 0.33. Some macro-oriented researchers (Keane, Rogerson) favor values closer to 0.5. Estimates below 0.1 or above 0.7 are quite rare for the intensive margin alone. My median sits at 0.3, reflecting the central tendency of the careful microeconometric and structural lifecycle literature, with meaningful uncertainty around it.

citations: MaCurdy (1981) - estimated Frisch elasticity around 0.2–0.45 using lifecycle male labor supply

runs.reasoningSummary: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity for prime-age employed U.S. workers is a well-studied but disputed parameter. Micro labor-supply studies (MaCurdy 1981 and followers) typically find values in the 0.1–0.5 range for men. Chetty (2012) argues that after correcting for optimization frictions, the micro estimate rises to ~0.54. Macro/lifecycle calibrations often use values of 0.5–1.0, but those partly reflect extensive margin or broader definitions. Restricting to the intensive margin for prime-age workers already employed, the bulk of the credible empirical distribution sits between roughly 0.2 and 0.6, with a median near 0.4. The upper tail extends to ~0.9 to capture higher macro-calibration values and measurement uncertainty.

citations: MaCurdy (1981, JPE) — structural lifecycle estimate of the intensive-margin Frisch elasticity, often cited around 0.1–0.45
Imai & Keane (2004, Econometrica) — finds substantially larger Frisch elasticities (~0.7) when human capital accumulation is modeled

runs.reasoningSummary: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity for prime-age U.S. workers has been debated for decades. Structural micro studies (MaCurdy 1981, Altonji 1986) tend to find low values, often 0.1–0.45. Imai & Keane (2004) find much larger values once human capital is modeled (~0.7). Macro calibrations (Hansen 1985, Rogerson & Wallenius) often use higher values but those include participation. Chetty's (2012) reconciliation suggests ~0.33 as a defensible intensive-margin estimate. The consensus range for prime-age intensive-margin Frisch is roughly 0.1–0.5, with 0.3 as a reasonable central value. I place modest probability mass below 0.1 (very low micro estimates) and above 0.6 (high structural estimates), yielding p05=0.05, p25=0.15, p50=0.30, p75=0.50, p95=0.75.

Female estimates tend to be higher but the target population is prime-age workers broadly. Macro calibrations sometimes use larger values (0.5\u20131.0), but those often blend in extensive-margin responses. Restricting to intensive margin only (hours conditional on working), the bulk of evidence clusters between 0.1 and 0.5. I center my median at 0.3, reflecting the midpoint of credible microeconometric estimates, with modest upward skew to accommodate macro-consistent corrections."}

Answer from your current memory and background knowledge only.
Do not use tools, files, the web, code, or external resources.
Do not try to reconstruct a literature review or search for a consensus estimate.
Report the belief you currently endorse.

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Frisch elasticity of labor supply
- Definition: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage, holding the marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses.
- Target interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity used in lifecycle or macro calibration
- Population/context: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
- Units: elasticity

Task:
1. Use exactly the target interpretation above. In `interpretation`, restate it briefly.
2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.
6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

Return valid JSON only with exactly this shape:
{
  "interpretation": "...",
  "point_estimate": <number>,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": <number>,
    "p25": <number>,
    "p50": <number>,
    "p75": <number>,
    "p95": <number>
  },
  "citations": ["..."],
  "reasoning_summary": "..."
}

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity for prime-age U.S. workers already in employment, holding marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses, used in lifecycle or macro calibration.
citations: MaCurdy (1981) – estimated Frisch elasticity around 0.1–0.45 using lifecycle panel methods for prime-age men
Pencavel (1986) – survey of male labor supply estimates suggesting intensive-margin elasticities in the 0.1–0.5 range
Chetty (2012, AEJ: Macroeconomics) – reconciliation paper arguing that micro estimates around 0.5 are consistent with macro evidence once adjustment costs and optimization frictions are accounted for
runs.reasoningSummary: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity for employed prime-age U.S. workers is a well-studied parameter. Micro-econometric estimates (MaCurdy 1981, Pencavel 1986) tend to cluster in the 0.1–0.5 range for men, with some studies finding up to ~0.75. Macro/lifecycle calibrations often use values of 0.5–1.0 to match business-cycle fluctuations. Chetty (2012) argues that a Frisch elasticity around 0.5 is a reasonable reconciliation. My median is 0.5, reflecting the midpoint between tight micro estimates and the higher values used in macro models, with the distribution skewed slightly rightward given macro-calibration usage.

Micro-econometric estimates (MaCurdy 1981, Pencavel 1986) tend to cluster in the 0.1\u20130.5 range for men, with some studies finding up to ~0.75. Macro/lifecycle calibrations often use values of 0.5\u20131.0 to match business-cycle fluctuations. Chetty (2012) argues that a Frisch elasticity around 0.5 is a reasonable reconciliation. My median is 0.5, reflecting the midpoint between tight micro estimates and the higher values used in macro models, with the distribution skewed slightly rightward given macro-calibration usage."}
